policy_text:
Effective June 1, 2026, all employees based in our San Francisco and New York offices are required to work from the office Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday each week. This policy applies to all departments and levels.

We believe in-person collaboration is essential to building the products our customers need and the culture we want to be known for. The era of indefinite remote flexibility is ending, and we are aligning with industry best practices.

Exceptions will be reviewed case-by-case at manager discretion. Employees who cannot meet the in-office requirement should discuss alternatives with their manager.

We are confident this change will accelerate execution, strengthen mentorship, and improve company performance. We appreciate your partnership in this transition.

parsed_policy.summary: San Francisco and New York office employees must work in-office Tuesday through Thursday each week starting June 1, 2026.

actions.content: I moved to San Rafael and bought a house during remote work — that's 75 minutes each way over the bridge. I've been leading a distributed team for two years with zero productivity issues, so forcing Tue/Wed/Thu in-office feels like we're optimizing for the wrong thing.

I was hired as remote from Portland four years ago, and this policy effectively changes my employment terms. With two kids who have special needs and require flexible scheduling, Tuesday-Thursday in SF isn't physically possible for me—are we talking about a formal exception proce

I know the Tue-Thu mandate is a shift, but honestly I think this is going to help us rebuild the mentorship culture we've been missing. Our newer reps need more facetime with senior folks, and I've seen attrition correlate with isolation.

Need to flag something before June — the Tue/Wed/Thu requirement creates a direct conflict with my Wednesday commitment to my parents' medical appointments in White Plains. My accounts don't need me in the city, and losing six hours to commuting those days will actually hurt my p

We need to talk about Tuesday-Thursday in-office. I'm in Sacramento now with a 90-minute commute each way and daycare pickup at 5:30 — this literally doesn't work, and you know I turned down Stripe because I was promised full flexibility.

Hey, need to flag something urgent — the Tuesday-Thursday mandate conflicts directly with my custody schedule. I do school pickup in Cobble Hill at 3:15pm those days, and there's no workaround without losing time with my kids.

I need to understand how this applies to me — I was hired as fully remote from Boulder three years ago, and my entire family is here. Moving to SF or maintaining two households isn't feasible with two young kids and my spouse's career.

The Tuesday-Thursday mandate wipes out my morning drop-off routine for both kids three days a week. I need to understand how the exception process actually works before June, because my current setup won't survive this.

The Tue-Thu structure is the right call — we're paying $180K/month for SF real estate and our cross-functional velocity has been slipping. This will tighten up collaboration and give us better line of sight on burn.

I need to understand how exceptions work here — I relocated my family to Boise two years ago when I was hired remote, and moving everyone back to SF isn't realistic with two kids under 5. Is there actually a path forward for me, or should I be looking at my options?

I was hired explicitly as remote from Portland to care for my parent post-surgery — that was the deal. I'm not in SF or NY, so does this even apply to me, or is there some reorg assumption I'm missing?
I was hired as fully remote from Austin and I'm already on the road 40% of the time for customers. There's no SF or NY office within 1500 miles of me — what's the path forward here?

I need to escalate something urgent — my offer letter explicitly guarantees remote work, and I'm based in Austin, not SF or NY. I bought a house here two years ago. How does this policy apply to remote hires with contractual remote agreements?

I was hired remote with explicit assurance this role didn't need office presence, and I'm in Sacramento with a 3-year-old. A 4+ hour daily commute three days a week isn't feasible for my family — can we talk about how exceptions actually work here?
I need to flag this now: with my Oakland commute and daycare pickup at 6pm sharp, three days in-office isn't going to work. What's the actual process for exceptions here?

I need to flag something before we cascade this further. The Tue-Thu mandate is going to hit caregivers and our Marin/East Bay cohort hard — I'm looking at 80 minutes each way plus a 3:30 pickup three days a week, and I'm not alone in that math.

The Tuesday-Thursday mandate doesn't work with my co-parenting schedule — I have the kids Wednesday nights and coordinate their after-school activities that day. Can we talk about what flexibility actually looks like here?

Honestly, the commute thing is going to be a problem. Frustrated.

themes.label: Commute strain
themes.description: Employees expect the change to create meaningful time and energy costs outside work.

themes.label: Caregiving burden
themes.description: Employees are worried the policy does not account for caregiving, medical, or accessibility constraints.

themes.label: Loss of flexibility
themes.description: Employees read the policy as reducing autonomy and flexibility they rely on to do good work.

recommendation.title: Move to a phased hybrid rollout with guaranteed exceptions
recommendation.rationale: The strongest theme is Commute strain. This recommendation reduces ambiguity, moves sensitive exceptions out of ad hoc manager discretion, and gives employees a visible feedback loop before the policy becomes permanent.
recommendation.suggested_rewrite:
Starting September 1, we will move to a hybrid collaboration model for office-assigned employees. Teams will use two shared anchor days per week for planning, mentorship, and customer-facing collaboration; any third in-office day is optional and set by each team based on the work.

Caregiving, medical, disability, commute-hardship, and previously remote role exceptions are guaranteed through People Ops, not manager discretion. June through August will be a ramp period with no attendance tracking while teams test schedules and share feedback. We will publish the business rationale, review pulse results after 30 and 90 days, and adjust the policy before making it permanent.

summary: Predicted -22 eNPS over 30 days. 10 LinkedIn updates expected. Top concern: Commute strain.
